text: Attention, for William James, was the hinge on which the whole of mental life swings. 'The faculty of voluntarily bringing back a wandering attention, over and over again, is the very root of judgment, character, and will,' he wrote. He distinguished voluntary attention — effortful, directed by intention — from involuntary attention, which is seized by novelty or intensity without our choosing. James suspected that the capacity for sustained voluntary attention was finite and exhaustible, a suspicion confirmed a century later when psychologists documented what they called 'ego depletion' — the measurable decline in self-control following prolonged cognitive effort.
question: What did James call the faculty he described as 'the very root of judgment, character, and will'?
